{{Short description|Change in wave frequency due to relative motion of source and observer}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Doppler effect
| image = Dopplerfrequenz.gif
| caption = A stationary source (top) vs. a moving source (bottom): wave fronts bunch up ahead of the moving source.
| discovered_by = [[Christian Doppler]] (1842)
}}

The '''Doppler effect''' (also known as the '''Doppler shift''') is the change in [[frequency]] of a [[wave]] in relation to an observer who is moving relative to the wave source. It is named after Austrian physicist [[Christian Doppler]], who described the phenomenon in 1842.

A common example is the change in pitch of a [[siren (alarm)|siren]] as an emergency vehicle approaches and then recedes. The effect applies to all types of waves, including [[sound]], [[light]], and [[water wave]]s.

== Sound waves ==
For sound waves in a medium, the observed frequency <math>f_{\text{obs}}</math> is:
:<math>f_{\text{obs}} = f_{\text{src}}\frac{v \pm v_{\text{obs}}}{v \mp v_{\text{src}}}</math>

where:
* <math>f_{\text{src}}</math> = source frequency
* <math>v</math> = speed of sound in the medium (≈343 m/s in air at 20°C)
* <math>v_{\text{obs}}</math> = observer velocity (positive toward source)
* <math>v_{\text{src}}</math> = source velocity (positive toward observer)

'''Sign convention (numerator):''' + when moving toward source; − when moving away.
'''Sign convention (denominator):''' − when source moves toward observer; + when source moves away.

== Light (relativistic Doppler effect) ==
{{Main|Relativistic Doppler effect}}
For electromagnetic waves, only relative motion matters (no medium required). The relativistic longitudinal Doppler shift is:
:<math>f_{\text{obs}} = f_{\text{src}}\sqrt{\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}}</math>
where <math>\beta = v/c</math>.

For <math>v \ll c</math>, this approximates to:
:<math>f_{\text{obs}} \approx f_{\text{src}}\left(1 + \frac{v}{c}\right)</math>

== Redshift and blueshift ==
* '''[[Redshift]]:''' Frequency decreases (wavelength increases) — source receding
* '''[[Blueshift]]:''' Frequency increases (wavelength decreases) — source approaching

In astronomy, the redshift <math>z</math> is defined as:
:<math>z = \frac{\lambda_{\text{obs}} - \lambda_{\text{src}}}{\lambda_{\text{src}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}} - 1</math>

== Applications ==

=== Astronomy ===
* Measurement of [[radial velocity|radial velocities]] of stars and galaxies
* Discovery of the [[expansion of the universe]] ([[Hubble's law]])
* Detection of [[exoplanet]]s via the [[radial velocity method]]
* [[Redshift]] surveys of galaxies

=== Radar and sonar ===
* [[Radar gun|Radar speed guns]] — measure vehicle speed from frequency shift
* [[Doppler radar]] — weather tracking, wind speed measurement
* [[Sonar]] — underwater object detection and speed measurement

=== Medicine ===
* [[Doppler echocardiography]] — blood flow measurement
* [[Doppler ultrasound]] — fetal heart rate monitoring

=== Other ===
* [[Laser Doppler velocimetry]] — fluid flow measurement
* [[Synthetic aperture radar]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Redshift]]
* [[Blueshift]]
* [[Relativistic Doppler effect]]
* [[Hubble's law]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Doppler effect| ]]
[[Category:Wave mechanics]]
[[Category:Astronomical spectroscopy]]
